findings.message: `derive_current_refs` still relies on the caller having mutated the process CWD before invocation, even though `repo_root` is carried in the command and the diff-source port does not receive it. That makes this usecase flow depend on ambient process state through `DryCheckDiffSource`/`GitDryCheckDiffGetter::discover()`: a caller with a different CWD, or concurrent code changing CWD, can list hunks from the wrong repository while fragments are normalized against `cmd.repo_root`. This is an implicit process dependency in the usecase orchestration boundary; bind the diff source to `repo_root` explicitly or pass the root through the port instead of relying on global CWD.

`PrReviewPollingService::poll` now has two new externally visible outcomes (`ZeroFindings` and exact-HEAD timeout recovery), but the added tests only cover `ReviewFound`, `Timeout`, and sleep cadence. There is no coverage for either `ZeroFindings` branch or the recovery path, so regressions in the main zero-findings logic can still ship unnoticed.

`resolve_dry_gate` now maps fragment-pipeline failures into `D4OrchestrationError::DryGate` at lines 596-599, but the test block only covers the enabled/disabled paths plus diff-base and corpus-meta failures. There is no assertion that a `fragment_pipeline` error propagates, so this new failure branch can regress unnoticed.

`PrReviewPollingService::poll` exposes several new `D4OrchestrationError::PrPolling` error paths (invalid trigger timestamp plus review/reaction/comment list and JSON parse failures), but the added tests only cover the success outcomes (`ReviewFound`, `ZeroFindings`, `Timeout`, recovery, and sleep cadence). None of the `Err` branches are asserted, so the new error mapping can regress without a failing test.

`poll` no longer enforces a wall-clock deadline; it stops after a fixed `max_iterations` count, so a slow GitHub API call can push the loop well past the caller's timeout. The original `Instant`-based check would have broken out once the deadline expired.

[libs/usecase/src/pr_review.rs:465-470,591] `poll` still reads wall-clock time inside the usecase via `Instant::now()` to derive and re-check the deadline. That makes timeout behavior depend on runtime time instead of an injected port or parameter, which breaks the usecase purity boundary and leaves the polling loop non-deterministic to test.

`emit` creates and opens the telemetry path with plain `create_dir_all`/`OpenOptions::open`, so a symlinked `telemetry_dir` component or pre-existing `telemetry.jsonl` symlink is followed instead of being rejected. This adapter is the infrastructure I/O boundary for the injected telemetry path; when it is wired to archived-track logs, a symlink under the trusted archive tree can redirect appends outside that tree instead of failing closed. Match the existing CN-04/scope_config_loader symlink-rejection pattern, or use a trusted-root guard plus no-follow open before writing.

Path containment is never enforced after deriving `canonical_root`, so a crafted `items_dir` like `../other/track/items` can escape the intended trusted root before `review.json` / `.commit_hash` are opened. `reject_symlinks_below` only rejects symlinks; it does not replace the `canonical_path.starts_with(&canonical_root)` check used in `scope_config_loader.rs`.

The same trusted-root gap exists in the ref-verify adapter: `items_dir_abs` is built and used without proving it stays under `canonical_root`, so `RefVerifyScopeResolver` / `RefVerifyCacheAdapter` can be pointed at an escaped directory tree via a relative `items_dir`.

`review_status` no longer verifies that `track/items/<track-id>` exists before reading `review.json` and `.commit_hash`. Because both stores treat missing files as empty/`None`, a missing or mistyped track directory can now fall through to `Approved`/`ApprovedWithBypass` instead of failing like the original `build_v2_shared` path. Restore the `track_dir.is_dir()` guard here.

`resolve_gate_path_context()` anchors git discovery to `items_dir` itself for absolute paths. If that path sits under a nested checkout, `SystemGitRepo::discover_from(items_dir)` can select the nested repo as the trusted root, so these adapters read `review-scope.json`, `review.json`, `.commit_hash`, and ref-verify caches from the wrong repository instead of the caller's repo root. Anchor discovery to the caller's repo root first, as the composition layer did.

`resolve_gate_path_context()` anchors git discovery on `SystemGitRepo::discover()` from the process cwd, so the gate adapters no longer root `.commit_hash` ancestry checks in the validated `items_dir`/`canonical_root`. If the command runs from another repo, or from outside a repo, `FsCommitHashStore::read()` can consult the wrong repository or fail for a valid project. The previous implementation wrapped the same read in `with_repo_cwd(&canonical_root, ...)` to keep this I/O bounded to the trusted root.

`items_dir_abs.canonicalize()` follows a symlinked `items_dir` root, but this helper never rejects that root with a `symlink_metadata().file_type().is_symlink()` check. Other infrastructure adapters explicitly refuse to use a symlinked `items_dir` before treating it as the trusted anchor, because `reject_symlinks_below()` only protects descendants. As written, a symlinked `items_dir` can become the gate's trust root.

`resolve_gate_path_context` only rejects a symlink when the leaf `items_dir` path itself is a symlink. A symlinked ancestor (for example `track/` under the caller repo) can still redirect the canonicalized path into a different subtree, and the adapter will then treat that subtree as the project root. This should reject symlinks along the full path below the caller's trusted root, matching the repo's existing `reject_symlinks_below` boundary checks.

The `absolute items_dir` branch stops anchoring to the current repo: any absolute path outside the caller repo is accepted as long as its derived project root is a git root. That means the adapter can be pointed at a different repository instead of failing closed under the caller's trusted root.

`reject_symlinks_below()` is only applied when `items_dir_abs.starts_with(&canonical_repo_root)`, so an absolute `items_dir` that reaches the repo through a symlink outside the repo can bypass the symlink check and still be accepted after `canonicalize()`. The repo’s own loader pattern (`scope_config_loader.rs`) rejects symlinks on the original path unconditionally before trusting the result.

`FsReviewGateStateAdapter::review_status` only re-anchors the `.commit_hash` lookup; the subsequent `ReviewCycle::evaluate_approval` still runs with the caller's ambient CWD, but `GitDiffGetter` and `SystemReviewHasher` discover the repo from that CWD. In nested-checkout or subdirectory invocations this can make the adapter read diff/hash data from the wrong repository instead of the validated `canonical_root`. Anchor the whole evaluation on `canonical_root` (the way `build_v2_shared` does) before calling `evaluate_approval`.

`OpenOptions::open(&lock_path)` will follow an existing `<db_path>.lock` symlink, so a caller can end up locking an attacker-controlled target outside the intended index sidecar. This is an infrastructure I/O boundary; add a `symlink_metadata(&lock_path)` check before opening and reject `file_type().is_symlink()` like the existing real-file boundary checks.

`read_spec_json` reads the caller-supplied `spec.json` with `std::fs::read_to_string` directly, so a symlink at the file or any parent component is followed before decoding. `spec.json` has existing real-file semantics in the repo (`FsSpecFileLoader` and `verify/spec_states.rs` reject symlinks before reads, matching the symlink-rejection boundary pattern covered by `scope_config_loader.rs` tests). This new adapter reintroduces an unguarded spec load path for `signal calc-spec-adr`; add the same `reject_symlinks_below`/trusted-root guard before reading.

`CodeFragmentExtractorAdapter::extract` only delegates to `extract_code_fragments` and never applies the CWD guard that the T007 briefing assigns to this adapter. The caller path now restores CWD before invoking the fragment pipeline, while the diff side still relies on `SystemGitRepo::discover()` being rooted at `repo_root`; with this implementation, discovery depends on the ambient process CWD and can diff the wrong repo or fail in nested/monorepo layouts. The adapter/port boundary needs to carry `repo_root` and restore the original CWD on all paths, or the CWD guard must remain at the caller.

`DryCorpusRootManifest` is a versioned on-disk DTO but it is deserialized directly without `#[serde(deny_unknown_fields)]` or a schema-version envelope. A future `dry-check-corpus-root.json` with `schema_version: 1` plus new fields would be accepted and those fields silently ignored instead of failing closed, which is a serde codec regression in this layer.

`validate_workspace_root()` canonicalizes `workspace_root` and only checks that the resolved path stays under `repo_root`; it never rejects a symlinked root itself. That lets a manifest point `workspace_root` at a symlink inside the repo and have `compute_corpus_fingerprint()` traverse the symlink target, so the fingerprint can describe a different tree than the manifest records. Existing root-anchored adapters in this crate reject symlinked roots before canonicalization, so this boundary should do the same.

`compute_corpus_fingerprint` hashes paths relative to the root you pass in, so using `workspace_root` here drops the repo-relative prefix that `dry write` includes when it stores the corpus fingerprint. For any scoped workspace (for example `scoped/` under the repo root), the approval path will compute a different fingerprint from the one written to coverage and will incorrectly treat the corpus as stale.

`/track:dry-check` now introduces a `skipped` terminal state at `.claude/commands/track/dry-check.md:15`, but the top-level orchestrator still documents only `completed` / `blocked` / `failed` for the DFP step in `.claude/commands/track/full-cycle.md:43-52`. That leaves the new opt-out outcome unhandled on the main `/track:full-cycle` path, so repositories with `dry-check.enabled: false` can still stall before Review instead of flowing through as intended.

Step 0a now short-circuits before the existing `track/<id>` branch check in Step 0b, so when `.harness/config/dry-check.json` is missing or `enabled:false`, `/track:dry-check` can return success even from a non-track branch. That bypasses the command's own branch guard and can let `/track:full-cycle` continue from the wrong workspace.

This reviewer prompt is part of the template distribution surface, but it tells future reviewers to cite "AC-05 of the track spec" as authority for the `Result<_, String>` rule. That acceptance criterion belongs to the current implementation track, not a durable ADR/convention file present in a fresh consumer checkout, so the shipped policy will contain an unresolvable reference. Replace it with a stable citation such as ADR D2 or state the typed-error rule directly.

The new `cli_driver` review prompt declares policy for `apps/cli-driver/`, but the scope loader is not wired to it: `.harness/config/review-scope.json` still has no `cli_driver` group, and `bin/sotp review files --scope cli_driver` reports `Unknown scope`. As a result, changes under `apps/cli-driver/**` will not dispatch to this briefing, so the new D2/D3/D4 driver rules are unreachable until the review-scope config adds a matching pattern and `briefing_file`.

`lib.rs` exposes the six new driver modules, but those module files are untracked and absent from `git diff`/the index. If this branch is submitted as-is, the tracked change leaves dead module references here and the future `cli-driver` crate flip will fail to find these modules. Add the six files to the change set or defer these `pub mod` lines until the files are tracked.

`format_review_summary_stub` does not preserve the existing `format_review_summary` render contract: the live helper includes `Inline comments: {count}` and an `Inline Comments:` section with each finding's location/body, but this staged helper only renders the review id, state, and body. When T021 wires `pr_review_cycle` to this helper, Codex review summaries with inline findings would stop showing the actionable comments, making non-zero PR reviews appear much less informative than today.

The audit bundle still encodes ADR 1328 as requiring a separate `cli-presentation` layer/crate for rendering, and the same assumption is repeated in the README remediation text. That contradicts the ADR added in this PR, which explicitly rejects the `cli-presentation`/4-layer split and places render inside the new `cli_driver` primary adapter. Because this bundle is cited as the evidence/remediation base for the track, following it would steer the implementation toward an architecture the governing ADR rejects; update the findings/remediation wording to move rendering out of `cli_composition` into `cli_driver`, not `cli-presentation`.

The audit bundle is internally inconsistent about the category for `dry_results`: the README lists `apps/cli-composition/src/dry.rs:437-492` under `pre-formatted-stdout-stderr-in-composer-methods` and describes it as presentation/rendering work, but the source JSON records the same finding as `multi-step-orchestration` even though its own description also says it is output-formatting/presentation logic. This makes the category counts/remediation mapping unreliable for the plan artifact: downstream readers cannot tell whether this finding is part of the render migration or orchestration migration.

The plan artifacts give conflicting `CliApp` public-method counts for the same god-facade evidence. ADR 1328 uses `51 メソッド/27 ファイル` as the baseline, while the audit README says approximately 140 public methods and the JSON contains both `50 pub fn methods total` and `140+ pub fn methods`. Because this count is cited as evidence for D2/D6(e) decomposition scope, leaving all four values in the plan makes the evidence base self-contradictory and undermines sizing for the `CliApp` dissolution work.

The audit JSON has two category assignments swapped relative to the findings' own semantics and the README: `signal_check_gate` is labeled `pre-formatted-stdout-stderr-in-composer-methods` even though its rule/description and README section classify it as multi-step orchestration, while `dry_results` is labeled `multi-step-orchestration` even though its rule/description and README classify it as presentation formatting. This leaves the audit bundle internally contradictory while preserving the category counts, so ADR 1328's D1/D3/D4 evidence map can point readers at the wrong remediation bucket. Align these JSON categories with the README/description semantics.

D2 says `PrimaryAdapter` gets a `NoRoleInMethodSignature` check to keep domain/usecase role types out of public method signatures, and D3 separately lists `UseCase`, `Interactor`, `Command`, `Query`, and `ApplicationService` as forbidden in CLI layers. The rule specified here only forbids `Entity`, `AggregateRoot`, `ValueObject`, `Repository`, and `SecondaryPort`, so a `cli_driver` public method could still expose usecase command/query/interactor types without this signature rule firing. The lint plan does not enforce its own boundary-purity requirement unless those usecase roles are included or the decision is narrowed consistently.

`T014` creates and wires `cli_driver` while explicitly deferring the `architecture-rules.json` flip to `T021`, but CN-06 requires the crate creation and architecture-rules update to land in the same commit. As written, the staged rollout cannot satisfy its own gate, so the plan needs either a single atomic task/commit here or a relaxed constraint.

`FsArchivedTrackTelemetryAdapter` is described as having both the telemetry file path and clock source injected as private fields, but the paired task definition hardcodes `new(telemetry_dir: PathBuf) -> Self` and still calls `chrono::Utc::now()` directly. That leaves the telemetry abstraction internally inconsistent about whether time is injected or sourced directly, so the adapter contract is not coherent as written.

`T005`-`T008` add delegation shims in `cli_composition`, but `T021` only removes render helpers and shims pointing to infrastructure adapters. Nothing deletes the D4 usecase-delegation shims, so the final state can still contain `cli_composition` invoke calls and miss `AC-03`/`CN-02`.

`T009` requires `apps/cli/Cargo.toml` to stop listing `chrono`/`serde_json` immediately after that commit, but `S8`/`T021` says the Cargo/deny/architecture dependency-graph changes happen atomically in the final flip. As written, the manifest edit is either unscheduled or split across commits.

`T005` requires `SignalGateInteractor::new()` to take injected ports, but the type catalogue models `SignalGateInteractor` as a zero-argument constructor and simultaneously says it holds injected ports as private fields. That is a direct contradiction: as written, the application service cannot satisfy the DI requirement without another catalog edit.

T001 specifies `FsArchivedTrackTelemetryAdapter` as a unit struct while also requiring `new(telemetry_dir: PathBuf) -> Self` and `emit` to write telemetry to that configured location. A unit struct cannot retain the injected telemetry directory; the type catalogue also describes the directory as injected into the adapter, so the implementation plan is internally contradictory and would leave the D8 telemetry extraction unimplementable as written.

AC-04 requires `pub struct CliApp;` to be absent, and task-coverage marks AC-04 as covered only by T013, but T013 explicitly allows a remaining `CliApp` compatibility shim when usages are not gone. That leaves the plan able to declare AC-04 covered while the forbidden type still exists; make T013 unconditionally remove the shim or move/extend AC-04 coverage to the final flip task that actually verifies removal.

The D8 telemetry path is catalogued only as `ArchivedTrackTelemetryPort`, a SecondaryPort, while T009 says the archived-track path delegates persistence directly to the infrastructure adapter. There is no usecase ApplicationService/Interactor for the driver to invoke, which conflicts with the ADR/spec model that `cli_driver` invokes use cases while secondary adapters remain behind usecase services. Add a telemetry application service or otherwise make the plan explicit that the driver is not calling a secondary port directly.

T009 requires routing the archived-track path through `cli_driver` and testing driver dispatch to the telemetry service, but the same plan does not stage the telemetry driver until T016 and explicitly defers live `cli_driver` wiring/imports to T021. As written, this makes T009 unexecutable without either violating the later atomic cutover constraint or referencing a driver path that does not exist yet. Defer the driver-dispatch part to T016/T021, or move T009 after the driver exists.

The usecase catalogue only defines SignalGate, SpecAdrSignal, and ArchivedTrackTelemetry application services/interactors, while the implementation plan also requires T007/T008 to extract diff-fragment, dry_check/dry_write, fixpoint dry-gate branching, and PR polling into usecase functions/application services. Because `functions` is empty and no additional application-service entries exist for those planned D4 extractions, the type catalogue and implementation plan disagree about the public usecase surface needed to satisfy IN-07/AC-07.

`OS-03` is mapped to `T022`, but `T022` only updates `.harness/custom/review-prompts/*` and never touches `knowledge/conventions/hexagonal-architecture.md` (see `impl-plan.json:131-132`). The spec entry at `spec.md:46` says the convention-doc edit is deferred to a separate post-implementation task, so the task-coverage claim that all 4 out-of-scope items are covered is incorrect.

OS-01 is the out-of-scope boundary for downstream 1420 work, but its `adr_refs` point directly at ADR 1420 D1-D3 instead of the 1328 decision that defines this track boundary. That makes the 1328 plan artifact trace to downstream requirements as if they were source decisions for this track, contradicting the intended 1328-only provenance/no-1420-leakage for spec elements.

T007 tells implementers to add `D4OrchestrationError` as part of `usecase::dry_check::fragment_pipeline`, but the type catalogue defines that same type with `module_path: "d4_orchestration"` and the contract map renders it under `usecase::d4_orchestration`. This leaves two competing owning modules for a shared T007/T008 error type, so the implementation can follow the task text and drift from the catalogue. Align the task text or catalogue to a single module path.

`OS-03` is now mapped to an empty task list, leaving one of the four out-of-scope spec elements without any implementation-plan coverage. The Phase 3 task-coverage artifact is supposed to provide a reverse mapping for every spec element, so this contradicts the plan artifact coverage contract and will leave the rendered spec with no task anchor for that item.

The final flip section says AC-09 confirms “all 7 adapters relocated,” but the spec’s AC-09 and the D7 task breakdown define the intended end state as six adapters relocated to infrastructure and `LazyBranchReader` removed via UFCS/SystemGitRepo cleanup. Leaving T021 with the “all 7 relocated” condition contradicts the detailed acceptance criteria and can send implementers to relocate `LazyBranchReader` even though the plan elsewhere says it must be eliminated.

GO-04 says all seven port-implementing adapters are moved to `libs/infrastructure`, but the detailed scope and acceptance criteria later say `LazyBranchReader` is removed instead while only the other six adapters are relocated. This leaves the top-level goal contradicting the implementation plan and can misdirect implementers toward creating a forbidden/obsolete infrastructure adapter for `LazyBranchReader`.

This delta is no longer limited to the T002 catalogue/signal normalization described in the briefing: `impl-plan.json` advances task bookkeeping (`T001` -> `done`, `T002` -> `in_progress`) and `plan.md` increments the resolved count to `1/22`. The brief explicitly said this scope should not change `impl-plan.json`, so the artifact set now includes out-of-scope plan-state updates rather than only the adapter-catalogue/signal edits.

`RecordingDryAgent`'s catalogue docs still describe the private fields as `inner agent + telemetry writer`, but the same artifact now declares `RecordingDryAgent::new(inner: A) -> (Self, TieredDryAgentRecorder)`. That constructor shape no longer injects a telemetry writer and instead exposes the recorder pair, so the catalogue gives readers the old ownership model. Update the docs to describe the inner agent plus per-tier recorders / returned `TieredDryAgentRecorder`.

T005 still describes `SignalGateInteractor::new()` as taking only `SignalLayerReader` plus `SignalGateMatrix`, but the current signal-gate implementation now injects three additional chain-runner ports (`AdrChainRunnerPort`, `SpecAdrChainRunnerPort`, `LayerChainRunnerPort`). That leaves the plan artifact out of sync with the actual constructor contract and hides a significant dependency change.

The `SignalGateInteractor` catalogue entry still says it holds only `SignalLayerReader` and `SignalGateMatrix` as private fields. The current source stores three more ports (`adr_runner`, `spec_adr_runner`, `layer_chain_runner`), so the type catalogue no longer reflects the real dependency set.

`SpecAdrSignalInteractor` is still described as doing the full `read->decode->evaluate->set->write` cycle, but the updated `SpecFileWriterPort` in the same file now returns/accepts `domain::SpecDocument` and explicitly says JSON codec lives in the adapter. That makes the layer boundary inconsistent: either the interactor still owns decode/encode, or the port docs/signatures need to be updated to match the adapter-owned codec split.

`SpecAdrSignalService` still describes the application service as running a `read->decode->evaluate->set->write` cycle, but the same catalogue now defines `SpecFileWriterPort` as returning and accepting `domain::SpecDocument` with JSON decode/encode handled inside the infrastructure adapter. That leaves the plan contradictory about which layer owns codec work, and an implementer following the service/task text could put serde/codec behavior back into the usecase interactor despite the updated port contract.

The T006 plan-artifact delta is supposed to leave `impl-plan.json` byte-identical and avoid scope creep, but this change mutates task state for T005 by marking it `done` and adding a commit hash, with the rendered plan reflecting the same resolved-count change. That makes this catalogue/signal regeneration update carry unrelated task-status changes, so the plan artifacts no longer describe a clean T006-only delta.

`CodeFragmentExtractorPort::extract` takes `std::path::Path` by value in the catalogue, which is not a valid implementable Rust parameter type because `Path` is unsized. The same invalid signature is mirrored in the rendered markdown. This needs to be `&Path` or `PathBuf` before the port can be implemented as described.

T007 is internally inconsistent about `dry_write` record aggregation. The plan and the interactor/service docs say T007 moves `dry_write` metrics aggregation into `DryFragmentPipeline*`, but `DryFragmentPipelineOutput` says `records_before/after/appended` are always 0 in this service and that aggregation lives in the dry-write path. The ownership of those counters needs to be made consistent across the plan artifacts.

The catalogue says `DryFragmentPipelineService` covers the `dry_check_approved` fragment-ref derivation moved out of `cli_composition`, but the implementation still derives `current_fragment_refs` inline in `apps/cli-composition/src/dry.rs` and only delegates the fixpoint `build_current_fragment_refs` path. This makes the plan artifact semantically overstate the completed D4 extraction and can let the plan signal Blue for a responsibility that still remains in the CLI composition layer. Update the catalogue/plan wording to match the current source, or complete the `dry_check_approved` delegation before marking this coverage as moved.

`DryFragmentPipelineInteractor` still claims its private dependencies are only existing dry-check/diff ports and that no new infrastructure adapter edge is introduced, but the same catalogue now adds `CodeFragmentExtractorPort`, adds `CodeFragmentExtractorAdapter`, and declares the interactor constructor dependency on `Arc<dyn CodeFragmentExtractorPort>`. That is a semantic contradiction in the plan artifacts and can mislead the implementation boundary for T007; update this docs field to acknowledge the new fragment-extractor secondary port/adapter edge.

`DryFragmentPipelineCommand` still tells callers to set the CWD to `repo_root`, but the paired `DryCheckDiffSource` change explicitly removes the ambient CWD dependency by taking `repo_root` as an argument and calling `SystemGitRepo::discover_from(repo_root)`. Those requirements conflict: the command no longer needs the caller to `chdir` just to satisfy the diff source, so the command contract is stale and will mislead the T007/T008 handoff.

`DryCheckDiffSource` is modified for T007 and its docs say the adapter now uses `SystemGitRepo::discover_from(repo_root)`, but the scoped artifacts never catalogue the corresponding diff-source adapter or its `impl DryCheckDiffSource` edge. The infrastructure catalogue only adds `CodeFragmentExtractorAdapter`, and the contract map shows `DryCheckDiffSource` as an unattached secondary port. That leaves the T007 pipeline wiring semantically incomplete in the plan artifacts.

`DryCheckDiffSource` is added as the T007 modified diff-source port, but this catalogue entry omits the `module_path` and `spec_refs` that the neighboring T007 usecase entries have. The rendered artifacts therefore place it directly under `usecase`, while the adapter side says it implements `usecase::dry_check::ports::DryCheckDiffSource`, and `usecase-types.md` marks the same port Cat-Spec 🔴. That leaves the repo-root diff-source change inconsistently located and ungrounded in the plan artifacts.

T006 still describes `SpecAdrSignalInteractor` as owning the read→decode→evaluate→set→write cycle, but the updated port contract moved JSON decode/encode behind `SpecFileWriterPort` and now returns/accepts `SpecDocument`. That means the task text is pointing implementers at the wrong layer boundary.

`DryFragmentPipelineOutput` is documented as carrying `records_*` aggregate counts, but the same T007 plan says those fields are compatibility counters fixed at 0 and that the real aggregation stays in `dry_write`. The DTO contract is internally inconsistent about whether these fields are meaningful metrics or placeholders.

`FixpointDryGateOutput` is still labeled as a DTO, but it carries `Arc<dyn DryCheckApprovalService + Send + Sync>` in `dry_approval`. That turns the output into a live dependency bundle rather than data-only output and conflicts with the declared DTO role for this artifact.

The output-role classification is internally inconsistent for the new D4 outputs: `SignalGateOutput` is tagged as `UseCase` here even though its own docs describe it as an output DTO, while `FixpointDryGateOutput` is tagged as `Dto` even though its docs explicitly say it should be `UseCase` because it carries a live `DryCheckApprovalService`. The rendered `usecase-types.md` / `contract-map.md` mirror the same swap, so the artifact set currently disagrees about whether these two outputs are DTOs or use cases.

`FixpointDryGateOutput` is catalogued as `UseCase` even though this same artifact defines it as the return payload of `FixpointDryGateService::resolve_dry_gate` and documents it as an output bundle. That classifies a service output as a use-case role in the generated catalogue/contract map, so the plan artifacts will publish the wrong TDDD role for the new API.

`PrRepoNwoPort` is declared as a new blue secondary port even though the same catalogue says `repo_nwo` is pre-resolved by the caller and carried on `PrReviewPollingCommand`, and `PrReviewPollingInteractor::new` only injects reviews/reactions/comments/sleep ports. That makes the plan artifacts contradict the intended boundary for PR polling: the usecase contract now advertises a repository-resolution dependency that production explicitly does not use, and the generated markdown/contract map will preserve that false port as completed.

The catalogue says `FixpointDryGateInteractor` owns the CWD guard, and the plan repeats that `FixpointDryGateService` owns the CWD guard. A CWD guard is process-global `std::env` state management, while the same T008 boundary models diff-base/corpus work behind secondary ports specifically to keep the usecase interactor free of `std::env`/I/O. Following this artifact would put infrastructure orchestration back into the usecase layer and violate CN-07; the plan/catalogue should assign CWD guard ownership to the composition/infrastructure caller and leave the service with explicit `repo_root` inputs.

`FixpointDryGateOutput` is classified as `Dto` here, but its own docs say it is intentionally not a DTO and describe it as a usecase-owned bundle carrying a service dependency. The generated markdown still lists it under DTOs, so the artifact set is internally inconsistent about this type's layer/classification.

T009 is now marked in progress even though this same plan says T001 must be completed before the cli-bin I/O removal and that S4 depends on T001, while T001 remains only in progress. That makes the task ordering self-contradictory: the plan is directing work on T009 before its required telemetry service/adapter prerequisite is resolved.

T013 names `dry_fix_runner` as one of the Group 3 command families, but the task only requires `DryCompositionRoot`, `PrCompositionRoot`, `ReviewCompositionRoot`, and `SemanticDupCompositionRoot`. Because the surrounding S5 plan establishes one per-context `CompositionRoot` per command family, this leaves the dry-fix-runner family without an explicit root while still claiming all remaining `CliApp` command logic will be removed in T013.

`plan.md` still renders T017 as todo (`[ ]`), but `impl-plan.json` marks T017 as `in_progress`. That leaves the generated plan out of sync with the canonical task state and misstates the active work item.

`plan.md` now marks the track as 22/22 resolved, but the regenerated usecase type view still shows yellow implementation signals for `ArchivedTrackTelemetryPort` and `ArchivedTrackTelemetryCommand`. Yellow means the type goal is still in progress / must be resolved before track end, so this is a semantic contradiction in the plan artifacts: the track is presented as complete while the surviving type catalogue says part of the usecase contract is not fully resolved.

`run_catalog_spec_chain` and `run_impl_catalog_chain` swallow `signal_check_layer_chain_with_strict(...)` errors by converting `Err(e)` into `CommandOutcome::failure(Some(e))` and then `Ok(SignalChainOutput)`. That means `SignalGateInteractor::run_gate` can no longer surface `SignalGateError::ChainExecutionFailed` for chains ②/③, so infrastructure/configuration failures are reported as ordinary gate failures instead of propagating to the outer `signal_check_gate` error mapping. The composition layer should propagate the `Err` here and only map it once at the CLI boundary.

`signal_check_gate` now derives `items_dir` and `TrackId` from the resolved `spec_json_path`, then passes only those into `SignalGateCommand`. That drops the exact `--spec-json` override path: an override like `/tmp/custom.json` is reinterpreted as `items_dir=/`, `track_id=tmp`, so chain ① will evaluate `/tmp/spec.json` or reject the parent name instead of the requested file. This path interpretation/validation belongs in the usecase boundary or should preserve the resolved spec path in the adapter/command, per the CLI-as-composition-root/usecase purity rule.

`max_iterations` is derived with floor division here, so any call where `timeout < interval` (or `interval == 0`) becomes `0` iterations and returns `Timeout` without ever polling GitHub. The previous deadline-based loop always performed an initial poll when `timeout > 0`, so this changes behavior for valid CLI inputs and can miss an already-completed review.

The new module-level `allow(dead_code)` keeps the old `poll_review_for_cycle` compiled in production even though it is no longer called. That helper still contains the full PR polling orchestration, GitHub calls, zero-findings branching, sleeps, and stderr output, so the T008 delegation is incomplete for this scope. Delete it or gate it behind `#[cfg(test)]` and move the remaining coverage to the usecase interactor tests.

`FsDryCorpusMetaAdapter` still owns the corpus-manifest policy: it branches on manifest presence, chooses `canonical_root` vs `repo_root`, swallows manifest-resolution errors into an `eprintln!` fallback, and computes the selected fingerprint. The briefing says this corpus manifest logic should move out of `cli_composition` with the dry-gate extraction; keeping it here makes composition more than flat adapter wiring. Move the policy into the usecase interactor and leave this layer with primitive adapter calls only.

The pre-extraction `poll_review_for_cycle` remains compiled in production; `#![allow(dead_code)]` only hides the warning. This function still performs the full PR polling orchestration in `cli_composition` by calling review/reaction/comment ports, applying zero-findings and timeout-recovery branching, and emitting polling side effects. T008 expects that flow to live only in `PrReviewPollingInteractor`, so this leaves duplicate production orchestration in the composition layer that can drift from the wired usecase path. Move it under `#[cfg(test)]` or retarget the tests to the usecase interactor.

AC-05 is still violated here: `VerifyCompositionRoot` keeps public helper methods returning `Result<Option<String>, String>` (`verify_ci_resolve_track_id` and the `_from_root` variant). Since `VerifyCompositionRoot` is publicly re-exported from `lib.rs`, these remain part of the cli_composition public surface after the CliApp removal. Convert them to `CompositionError` or make them crate-private so callers do not keep depending on stringly typed composition errors.

AC-05 also remains open in `GitCompositionRoot::current_branch_track_id_strict`: the method is public on the re-exported composition root and still returns `Result<Option<String>, String>`. This leaves a public string-error boundary in cli_composition despite the typed `CompositionError` migration, so the helper should either return `CompositionError` or stop being public.

`telemetry_emit_archived_track_subcommand` constructs the archived-track interactor and immediately calls `emit` inside `cli_composition`. Per the CLI composition policy, this layer should only wire the adapter/interactor graph and inject it into a driver; invoking the use case here is an invoke leak that belongs in `cli_driver` or another driving adapter.

`format_report` assembles the user-facing telemetry report text inside `cli_composition`. Rendering and output formatting are `cli_driver` responsibilities under ADR D2/D3, so this composition root should wire the report dependency into a driver/formatter rather than building stdout strings itself.

The production rustdoc still documents the old `ArchivedTrackTelemetryCommand { subcommand }` constructor shape. The implementation now supplies `track_id`, so this public wiring comment should be updated to include both fields to avoid documenting the stale archived telemetry contract.
